data_in.description: MassBank_example.msp file bundled with MetaboAnnotatoR package

data_out.description: Per-spectrum CSV library entries with positive/negative ionization mode suffixes

domain_knowledge: MSP (NIST MS Search) format stores spectral metadata and peak lists; conversion to CSV requires extraction of compound name, precursor m/z, retention time, and peak intensity tuples with proper ionization mode notation.

Peak-picking noise threshold (0.005) filters spectral peaks below relative intensity 0.5%, while marker peaks threshold (0.1) identifies diagnostic fragment ions representing ≥10% base peak intensity.
Positive ([M+H]+) and negative ([M-H]−) ionization mode designations must be retained in library entry identifiers to enable correct spectral matching during annotation workflows.
MassBank is a curated public MS/MS spectral database with standardized metadata; the bundled example subset validates import integrity without requiring external network access.

direct_checks: verify file 'MassBank_example.msp' exists in package root or data/ directory
script_runs: execute mspToLib function with inputs={MassBank_example.msp, noise=0.005, mpeaksScore=0.9, mpeaksThres=0.1} without errors
file_exists: output CSV library file(s) are created with naming pattern matching per-spectrum convention
file_format_is: output CSV contains expected column headers for MS/MS library format (e.g., precursor_mz, fragment_mz, intensity, or equivalent)
contains_substring: output filenames or metadata contain ionization mode suffix (e.g., '_pos' or '_neg') for each spectrum entry
row_count_equals: at least one CSV output row per input spectrum from MassBank_example.msp (robust to parameter choices in noise/threshold range)
verify output CSV structure is consistent with documented per-spectrum library entry format — no malformed rows or truncated records
expert_review: confirm peak-picking thresholds (noise=0.005, mpeaksScore=0.9, mpeaksThres=0.1) produce chemically reasonable fragment ion lists (e.g., no spurious low-intensity artifacts, retention of true fragment ions)
assess positive/negative mode suffix assignment accuracy — verify ionization mode annotations in output match input spectra metadata
evaluate whether per-spectrum CSV entries conform to MassBank or standard metabolomics library format conventions

[other] The mspToLib function reads and converts spectra records from .msp files into CSV library entries stored in a user-defined directory, with a 'positive' or 'negative' mode suffix added to each file name to facilitate organisation of custom libraries; occurrence scores are attributed to peaks above mpeaksThres threshold and noise level using default parameters (noise=0.005, mpeaksScore=0.9, mpeaksThres=0.1).: 'Use function *mspToLib* to read and convert the spectra records from the .msp files into library entries (.csv files), which will be stored in the user-defined library directory (*LibDir*). A'
[methods] MassBank_example.msp file bundled with MetaboAnnotatoR package: 'There is an example feature table in MetaboAnnotatoR (*targetTable.csv*)'
[methods] Per-spectrum CSV library entries with positive/negative ionization mode suffixes: 'MS/MS spectrum of D-Pantothenic Acid [M+H]+ adduct from MassBank'
[intro] MetaboAnnotatoR: 'MetaboAnnotatoR is designed to perform metabolite annotation of features from LC-MS All-ion fragmentation (AIF) datasets'
[intro] R: 'To install this package, start R (version "4.5.0" or higher)'
[discussion] No changelog documenting version history, updates, or parameter changes: 'No changelog found.'

cmd: library(MetaboAnnotatoR); msp_file <- system.file('extdata', 'MassBank_example.msp', package='MetaboAnnotatoR'); lib <- mspToLib(msp_file, noise=0.005, mpeaksScore=0.9, mpeaksThres=0.1); write.csv(lib, file='mspToLib_output.csv', row.names=FALSE)
executable.grounding_notes: Unverified by the executable guard — command runs inline code (-c/-e); its API calls cannot be validated against package files. Treat this command as a best-effort inference; verify the entry point and APIs against the real code before running.

expected_outputs: Per-spectrum CSV library entries with positive/negative ionization mode suffixes
finding: The mspToLib function reads and converts spectra records from .msp files into CSV library entries stored in a user-defined directory, with a 'positive' or 'negative' mode suffix added to each file name to facilitate organisation of custom libraries; occurrence scores are attributed to peaks above mpeaksThres threshold and noise level using default parameters (noise=0.005, mpeaksScore=0.9, mpeaksThres=0.1).
inputs: MassBank_example.msp file bundled with MetaboAnnotatoR package
methodology_summary: Load MSP spectral library from MetaboAnnotatoR package bundle.
Parse spectral records and extract compound metadata (name, m/z, RT) and peak intensity lists.
Apply noise threshold (0.005) and marker peak threshold (0.1) to filter and rank spectral peaks.
Append ionization mode suffixes ([M+H]+ or [M-H]−) to library entry identifiers based on precursor charge annotation.
Export filtered and annotated spectra as per-spectrum CSV rows with mode-suffixed headers.
Validation: Verify CSV row count matches input spectrum count, mode suffixes are present and consistent, and peak columns are numeric and within 0–100 relative intensity range.
missing_information: No changelog documenting version history, updates, or parameter changes
research_question: Does the mspToLib function correctly convert MS/MS spectra from .msp library files into individual CSV library entries with appropriate positive/negative ionisation mode suffixes when applied with default peak-picking parameters?

workflow_description: 1. Load the MassBank_example.msp file from the MetaboAnnotatoR package using mspToLib function. 2. Apply default peak-picking parameters: noise threshold of 0.005, marker peaks score of 0.9, and marker peaks threshold of 0.1. 3. Generate per-spectrum CSV library entries with mode-specific suffixes (positive/negative ionization mode designations). 4. Verify output file format and completeness of spectral annotations.
